data.title: Arista Networks May See Growth Accelerate In 2027
data.description:
Arista Networks, Inc. (NYSE:ANET) reported a strong end to FY24 with revenue coming in well beyond the management-guided range at $7b. Though management guided 17% growth for FY25, I believe guidance may be relatively conservative given the large capital outlay across the hyperscalers for compute and data center build-outs. Across the four major hyperscalers, I’m forecasting $313b in capital investments for FY25. Given the positive growth outlook for Arista, I am reiterating my BUY rating for ANET shares with a price target of $113/share at 14.81x FY26 price/sales.

Arista Networks Operations

Arista closed FY24 with exceptionally strong growth driven by the drive for technology upgrades in support of GenAI. Much of the growth can be attributed to Arista’s 100, 200, and 400G switches with the firm now commanding over 40% of the market share for these products.

Management is anticipating that its 800G Ethernet will emerge as an AI back-end cluster in 2025. One factor that management brought up in the Q4’24 earnings call was that the cycle time has shortened from 3-5 years when upgrading from 100G to 400G, to two years when upgrading from 400G to 800G. This could positively impact Arista as it rolls out 1.6T closer to FY27. Given the shorter cadence, Arista may realize an uplift in revenue growth in FY27 as it rolls out the next generation of data center switches. I believe that the timing may also fit within the growth trajectory of data centers as construction ramps up.

As a result, management is guiding top-line growth to continue to remain strong at the high end of their guidance at 17% for FY25. Growth will primarily be driven by cloud, AI, enterprise, and CSPs.

Services & subscription software’s contributions grew to 18.3% in Q4’24, likely the result of customers gravitating towards Arista’s zero-trust and AI software offerings.

Arista realized substantial growth in its CloudVision subscription offering, adding 350 new customers in 2024 alone. CloudVision is Arista’s network-as-a-service platform that provides interconnectivity across multiple domains with zero-touch network operations. CloudVision contributed roughly 17% of the firm’s total revenue in FY24.

Arista is also realizing good customer attachment in its AI for networking software, Network Data Lake (NetDL). NetDL addresses automation and security challenges with AI/ML. Accordingly, NetDL contributed $1b in net revenue in FY24 for software and subscriptions.

CSPs and “AI titans” contributed 48% of Arista’s net revenue as data center capacity was further expanded. Accordingly, three of the four major CSPs are expected to invest heavily in compute capacity in FY25, with a cumulative investment of 100,000 Nvidia GPUs, leading to strong business for Arista as it relates to the back-end network. Arista is currently migrating one of its CSP customers from InfiniBand, likely to Ethernet, for improved data rates and will bring this customer into production in 2026.

Arista recognized significant customer concentration with Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) and Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) accounting for 20% and 14.6% of revenue, respectively. Though this may be considered a risk, I believe that growth from each of these customers remains durable and has the capacity to expand.

As outlined in my recent report covering NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), I’m forecasting a 39% increase in capital investments across the hyperscalers. This includes Meta Platforms investing upwards of $65b and Microsoft at around $62b in FY25. Investments across the hyperscalers will primarily go towards compute capacity and data centers. Network connectivity will likely be a component of the capital outlay for the coming fiscal year.

Arista Networks Financial Position

Arista reported a strong end to FY24 with revenue coming in at $1.93b in Q4’24, realizing 25.3% year-over-year growth, exceeding management’s guidance of $1.85-$1.90b. For eFY25, management is guiding 17% top-line growth for $8.2b in net revenue.

Arista also realized significant margin expansion in FY24 as a result of improved supply chain and inventory management. Looking forward to FY25, management guided for higher investments in R&D, sales, and towards scaling the company. This will likely bring Arista’s adjusted operating margin back in line with its historical performance at 43-44%, down from 48% in FY24.

Looking to Q1’25, I’m forecasting Arista to generate $1.96b in net revenue with an adjusted EPS of $0.56/share. Despite my forecast for robust revenue growth in FY25, I believe margins may compress to a certain degree as management focuses on scaling the business to support its growth trajectory. Management also anticipates its effective adjusted tax rate to climb back to 21.5% in FY25, potentially resulting in a tighter net margin.

Days inventory declined to 267 days in Q4’24, down from 318 days in the year-ago quarter. Though days of inventory on hand declined, the nominal value of inventory increased sequentially as a result of Arista purchasing chips related to new product releases and AI deployments.

Management noted that tariffs may become a headwind if imposed. There have been suggestions that a 25%+ tariff on semiconductors may be announced as soon as April 2, 2025. Tariffs could potentially impact the cost of sales in the coming quarters if imposed. This may also drive near-term growth if customers seek to purchase equipment prior to potential price increases that may result from tariffs.

Arista ended FY24 with $8.3b in cash & equivalents and marketable securities, and $2.6b in working capital on the balance sheet. Arista repurchased $423.6mm shares in FY24 and has $921mm remaining in its share repurchase authorization. Though management didn’t necessarily disclose any changes to its capital deployment plans, they mentioned that Arista will continue investing in growth, expanding R&D, sales, and G&A in FY25. Management also noted that M&A is the least likely use of capital for FY25.

Arista deployed $32mm in capital investments in FY24. Management noted in the Q4’24 earnings call that the firm began construction work to expand its Santa Clara facility. This project is going to drive Arista’s capital outlay to grow to roughly $100mm in FY25, just over 3x the capital outlay in FY24.

Risks Related to Arista Networks

Bull Case

Arista is in the early innings of a large data center construction and compute deployment cycle as the hyperscalers commit hundreds of billions of dollars to building out capacity. Larger compute clusters can lead to stronger growth for Arista in its GPU interconnects and data center switches, and Ethernet.

Bear Case

Analysts are becoming increasingly skeptical of the need for large compute capacity following the release of the DeepSeek-V3 models. This can potentially lead to a lower growth trajectory for AI infrastructure and the need for higher-power networking equipment.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

Arista completed a four-for-one stock split on December 4, 2024, in order to broaden its investor base and allow for smaller investors to participate in trading ANET shares. It should be noted that stock splits do not impact the company’s valuation and financial performance aside from EPS reporting due to the higher float. Otherwise, valuation metrics like price/earnings are not impacted due to the application of shares to the share price as part of the company’s market cap and EPS as part of the company’s net earnings.

As of Q4’24, Arista had $921mm remaining in its share repurchase authorization.

ANET shares currently trade at a significant premium to its peer networking equipment competitors. I believe this premium is justified given Arista’s competitive niche in data center networking equipment as opposed to more dominant offerings for telecom or campus networking. Given the strong growth trajectory for data centers, Arista may realize substantial growth in the coming years, especially as customers transition from 800G to 1.6T switches.

Using an internal valuation model based on my sales forecast and ANET shares’ historical trading premiums, I believe shares should be priced at $113/share at 14.81x FY26 price/sales. Looking further out to FY27, I believe the market may price shares higher as Arista realizes a ramp-up of 1.6T switches. Though this is a few years forward, I believe shares will reflect incremental sales growth as a result of the volume sales of the switches. Given the strong outlook, I am reiterating my BUY rating for ANET shares.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market cap and relative multiple.

data.title: Why I'm Buying The Panic: Maintaining Palantir With A Buy
data.description:
Palantir Technologies Inc. (NASDAQ:PLTR) dropped over 12% on Wednesday, and the stock closed at $112.06 per share at the end of the trading day. It didn’t stop there, the stock is down as much as 13% since the market opened Thursday. To some, this might be the Palantir bubble finally popping, but to me, it’s a buying-the-panic opportunity.

The stock crumbled on two announcements

1. On Tuesday, Palantir’s regulatory filing disclosed that CEO Alex Karp plans to sell around 10 million shares of company stock in the next six months, as seen below. Karp adopted Rule 10b5-1 with the SEC to sell up to 9,975,000 shares through September of this year, netting him $1.23 billion at the price of $123. However, the filing revealed that Karp canceled a previous plan of selling 48.9 million shares worth up to $6.01 billion. So, while this looks bad at face value, it's not that detrimental when you put it into context.

2. On Wednesday, the Washington Post reported that Secretary Pete Hegseth ordered both senior Pentagon leaders and other military brass to prepare for a defense budget reduction of 8% annually over the next five years. The market is pricing in the effects this has on Palantir’s defense contracts, but I think the company won't be as exposed as investors fear. According to Hegseth, the proposed cuts aim at cutting “low-impact items,” including diversity, equity and inclusion, climate change studies, and other “woke programs.” For context, Palantir provides AI solutions and data analytics for both government and commercial entities and has several government contracts with the Department of Defense and the US Army. So, not only do I think the market overreacted to the news, but I think a big misunderstanding happened. If the budgets are being cut on some “woke” programs, who’s to say this won't translate to more strategic budget spending that prioritizes Palantir?

Hegseth ordered the cuts to be drawn up by Monday, with a list of 17 categories that the Trump administration wants exempted, including modernization of nuclear weapons and missile defense, one-way attack drones, and others. The Pentagon budget for 2025 is $850 billion, and the money saved could be “realigned” to pay for new priorities, including the Iron Dome for America, really an expansive missile defense system, which Trump said could shoot down incoming missiles from space. The cut makes up $50 billion of cuts in next year’s budget. If adopted fully, the cuts could go up to tens of billions of dollars each year for the next five years and would mark the largest effort to cut Pentagon spending since 2013. It’s still unclear on where the savings would be spent.

Karp and Musk: In bed?

All this is part of a series of measures Trump has been taking to slash government spending and the number of federal government workers. Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) CEO Elon Musk was appointed by Trump to oversee that effort through the DOGE, AKA the Department of Government Efficiency. In its first week, DOGE canceled $420 million worth of federal government contracts, and while not everyone is a fan, Karp is. According to Karp on the company’s last earnings call, in response to a question about DOGE, “We love disruption, and whatever is good for America will be good for Americans and very good for Palantir.” Since then, on different occasions, Karp expressed support for Musk, calling him the “most important builder in the world” and saying that the progressive left should be saying, “OK, Elon, you’re clearly the most qualified person in the world to do something like this. We want a dialog with you about what you’re doing, how you’re doing.’”

Earlier this month, Palantir bolstered its AIP by integrating Elon’s Grok, a large language model (“LLM”) developed by Musk’s AI startup xAI, known for its advanced capabilities in complex reasoning and coding, among other capabilities. It’s now joining other LLMs, including Meta Platforms, Inc.’s (META) Llama, Gemini, GPT-4, and Anthropic’s Claude on Palantir’s AIP platform. This further allows Palantir to cater to a broader range of client needs and sets it aside from competition with Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) and the likes of it.

Valuation has always been a point of concern, so how does it look going forward?

The stock isn't cheap and hasn't been for a while, and the bears had that hanging over Palantir for the longest time. But this isn’t news. Historically speaking, Palantir’s valuation multiples have compressed fast, and this might be an instance of that, with the stock down from $29 in September 2021 to under $9 by May 2022, with revenue multiple down from 35x to 10x, if even.

Palantir isn’t for the faint-hearted. The stock has a consensus hold rating among 18 Wall Street analysts, with three buy, 10 hold, and five sell recommendations, as seen below.

According to data from Refinitiv, Street analysts are cautious about the stock, and for good reason. I’m not saying the stock run-up wasn’t concerning; I’m saying the market overreacted to the news was, although I'm counting it as a blessing in disguise. Around 8.7% of street analysts give the stock a strong buy, 13% a buy, the majority of 56.5% a hold, and the rest a sell and a strong sell.

In terms of PT mean and median, the upward trend doesn’t necessarily mean a good thing, with the stock still trading higher than its PT mean and median at current levels. The PT median was at $39 in November, up slightly to $41 and $42 in December and January, respectively, and it currently stands at $95. Similarly, the PT mean was at $37.7 in November, up to $44 in December, $47 in January, and is currently at $90.8.

According to data from Seeking Alpha, the stock is currently priced at a premium, with forward P/E at 202.8, 689% higher than the sector median of 25.6, and the EV/Sales, similarly at 68.2, significantly higher than the sector median at 3.37.

Going Forward

I think if history taught us anything, it’s that Palantir always rebounds. Thus, I think the stock has more upside ahead for the rest of the year. I advise investors to add on the pullback, as I believe the stock is more attractive at current levels. According to Wedbush analysts led by Dan Ives, who maintained the $120 price target, “we believe this sell-off represents another opportunity,” and I share the sentiment.

data.title: uniQure: Gene Therapy Specialist Looks Set For Upside As Huntington's Approval Beckons
data.description:
Investment Overview - A Brief History Of uniQure To Present Day

This is my first time providing coverage of uniQure (NASDAQ:QURE) for Seeking Alpha, however it feels like a good time to be picking up the story, as I will explain in this post.

The gene therapy specialist, based in Amsterdam, Netherlands, completed its initial public offering in 2014m raising ~$91.8m via the issuance of 5.4m shares priced at $17 per share.

The company secured approval in Europe for Glybera (alipogene tiparvovec) in 2012. The therapy was approved to treat the ultra-rare disease hereditary lipoprotein lipase deficiency (LPLD), and was described in EU documentation as follows:

Alipogene tiparvovec contains the human lipoprotein lipase ("LPL") gene variant LPLS447X in a vector. The vector comprises a protein shell derived from adeno-associated virus serotype 1 (AAV1), the Cytomegalovirus (CMV) promoter, a woodchuck hepatitis virus posttranscriptional regulatory element and AAV2 derived inverted terminal repeats. Alipogene tiparvovec is produced using insect cells and recombinant baculovirus technology.

uniQure priced Glybera at ~$1.4m, but the drug was refused authorization in the US, and was a commercial failure, with too small an addressable market, and was withdrawn from the market in 2017. uniQure switched its attention to developing a therapy for hemophilia B, etranacogene dezaparvovec, which was approved in the US in November 2022, and in Europe in February 2023 as Hemgenix.

In June 2020, uniQure had agreed to sell the rights to market and sell Hemgenix to Pennsylvania Pharma CSL Behring, a subsidiary of CSL Limited, headquartered in Australia. The deal involved an upfront payment of $450m, up to $1.6bn of milestone payments, and "double digit royalties ranging up to a low-twenties percentage of net sales".

uniQure has earned ~$155m in milestone payments from the deal to date (source: 2023 annual report / 10-K submission), and agreed to sell rights to its lowest royalty tier to HemB SPV, L.P in exchange for a $375m payment in May 2023, although it maintains it is still eligible for up to $1.3bn in milestones. Across the first nine months of 2024, however, royalties received from CSL amounted to just $5.2m. In August this year, CSL Behring reported it had treated just 12 patients to date with Hemgenix, which has a list price of ~$3.5m.

In 2019, uniQure stock hit an all-time high of >$75 per share as it was revealed the company may be exploring a potential sale, however the sale of the Hemgenix rights put an end to that speculation, and by mid-2022 shares had sunk to <$15 per share, and by mid-2024, <$5 per share.

AMT-130's Huntington's Disease Approval Opens Up In Late 2024

uniQure's main area of focus since selling Hemgenix rights has been its candidate to treat Huntington's Disease, AMT-130, which the company describes (in its Q3 2024 quarterly report / 10-Q submission) as:

our novel gene therapy candidate for the treatment of Huntington’s disease, which utilizes our proprietary, gene-silencing miQURE platform and incorporates an AAV vector carrying a micro ribonucleic acid (“miRNA”) specifically designed to silence the huntingtin gene and the potentially highly toxic exon 1 protein fragment

It was AMT-130 news that sent uniQure soaring in December last year, from ~$6 per share, to ~$18 per share - a gain of ~200%. There was no data to share. However, management announced that it had "reached agreement with the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) on key elements of an Accelerated Approval pathway for AMT-130".

According to a press release:

As part of uniQure’s Regenerative Medicine Advanced Therapy (RMAT) Type B meeting held in late November, the FDA agreed that data from the ongoing Phase I/II studies, compared to a natural history external control, may serve as the primary basis for a BLA submission under the Accelerated Approval pathway, avoiding the need for an additional pre-submission study.

The FDA also agreed that cUHDRS may be used as an intermediate clinical endpoint and that reductions in neurofilament light chain (NfL) measured in cerebrospinal fluid ("CSF") may serve as supportive evidence of therapeutic benefit in the application for accelerated approval.

This is good news, as in July, uniQure shared positive data from its US and European Phase 1/2 study of AMT-130, as follows (Source: Q3 earnings press release):

At 24 months, the data demonstrated a statistically significant, dose-dependent slowing in disease progression measured by the composite Unified Huntington’s Disease Rating Scale (cUHDRS) in patients receiving the high dose of AMT-130 compared to a propensity score-weighted external control (p=0.007), as well as a statistically significant reduction of neurofilament light chain (NfL) in cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) in patients dosed with AMT-130 compared to baseline (p=0.02). AMT-130 continued to be generally well-tolerated with a manageable safety profile across both doses.

To get to this stage with AMT-130, uniQure has had to overcome an FDA hold imposed on the Phase 1/2 study by the FDA, due to a case of hepatocellular carcinoma ("HCC") diagnosed in one patient, resolved in April 2021, which led to a postponement of a higher dose study, and then, in June 2023, 24-month follow-up data which showed a temporary increase in Neurofilament Light Chain ("NfL"), a biomarker of neurodegeneration.

Today, however, it seems as though uniQure has a clear path to approval, after positive discussions with the FDA. Analysts have formerly speculated that AMT-130 could drive revenues of >$2bn, which, given the company's current market cap of $716m, suggests further progress towards this goal ought to drive further upside.

Steps To Securing Long-Awaited Approval For AMT-130 In 2025

In a January corporate presentation, uniQure maps out its plans for AMT-130 in 2025, as per the slide below:

According to uniQure's latest 10-Q:

We are currently conducting a multi-center randomized, controlled Phase I/II clinical trial for AMT-130 in the U.S. (“US study”) as well as an open-label Phase Ib/II study in Europe with the same early-manifest criteria for Huntington’s disease as the U.S. study (“European study”).

We completed the enrollment of all 26 patients in the first two cohorts of our US study in March 2022 and the enrollment of 13 patients in the two cohorts of our European study in June 2023.

In November 2023, we initiated patient dosing in a third cohort of up to 12 patients across both U.S. and U.K. sites to further investigate both doses of AMT-130 in combination with perioperative immunosuppression. Patient enrollment is ongoing in this cohort with a focus on evaluating near-term safety and tolerability.

Comparing its data from these first two studies to a historical control data set of 154 patients from three former natural history studies, who met the criteria for its own studies, uniQure was able to show that at the higher dose, AMT-130 was able to show "an 80% slowing of disease progression (p=0.007)", and at the lower dose, "a 30% slowing of disease progression (p=0.21)."

The company was also to conclude that AMT-130 had a "manageable safety profile at both doses", with "no new AMT-130 serious adverse events reported. In short, based on the totality of evidence, and the positive outcome of recent meetings with the FDA, it's hard to see how the agency could reject uniQure's Biological License Application ("BLA"), and how it could subsequently fail to approve the drug at least on efficacy and safety grounds.

Adding to the likelihood of approval is the fact that the FDA approved Biogen's (BIIB) Qalsody in the indication of SOD1-mutated amyotrophic lateral sclerosis ("ALS") based on an NfL endpoint.

Market & Competition

According to the Mayo Clinic website, "no treatments can alter the course of Huntington's disease". It is an inherited disease caused by a faulty "HTT" gene that affects cells in the brain, affecting movement, thought and feeling, that usually begins to exhibit symptoms when patients are between 30–50 years of age. The disease may affect as many as 40k people in the US alone.

Therapies approved to treat movement disorders (tardive dyskinesia) caused by Huntington's include Teva Pharmaceuticals' (TEVA) Austedo, and Neurocrine Biosciences' (NBIX) Ingrezza. The former is expected to earn >$2bn of revenues in 2025, while the latter earned $2.33bn of revenues in 2024. Danish Pharma Lundbeck's Xenazine is another approved HD therapeutic.

The advantages of AMT-130 as a "one-and-done" gene therapy that may require a single dose via an MRI-guided brain surgery seem clear, and may position AMT-130 as the best available therapy, if approved, although there are rival drugs that are also close to the finish line, namely Spanish Pharma Prilenia's pridopidine, which has already been submitted for approval in Europe, with a decision likely in the second half of this year, and Wave Life Sciences (WVE) WVE-003.

Wave states in its Q3 earnings press release that:

WVE-003 is expected to address approximately 40% of the HD population (potential $5 billion commercial opportunity), and up to 80% of patients with HD may be addressed in the future with other SNP-targeted candidates (potential $10 billion commercial opportunity).

FDA is open to Wave’s plan to evaluate biomarkers, including caudate atrophy, as an endpoint to assess HD progression with the potential to predict clinical outcome. Planning is underway for a global, potentially registrational Phase 2/3 study of WVE-003, including finalization of key aspects of design.

This suggests to me that Wave is not nearly so close to securing approval for WVE-003 as uniQure is for AMT-130, while we may also note that pridopidine failed a Phase 3 study in 2023, missing its primary endpoint of improvement on cUHDRS, although after excluding patients on other medications, the drug did achieve a statistically significant result, the company says.

In summary, my conclusion would be that AMT-130 is not only potentially superior to currently approved drugs, which fail to address the root cause of the disease, but also has the best chance of being the next approved drug to treat HD. The sales of existing drugs, and the numbers posited by Wave seem to underline the fact that if approved, AMT-130 has the opportunity to achieve 'blockbuster" (>$1bn revenues per annum) sales, and perhaps substantially more over the long term.

Concluding Thoughts - Hurdles Still To Overcome, But Bull Case Looking Strong

With uniQure's current market cap valuation being ~$715m, and with peak sales expectations for AMT-130 being ~$2bn, it is hard to escape the conclusion that the company is substantially undervalued.

As of the end of Q3, uniQure reported a cash position $251m, with $183m of investment securities, and a nine-month net loss of ~$(100m). In January, the company announced an equity offering at $17 per share, raising $75m. The company has racked up an accumulated deficit of >$1bn, and stock currently trades below the 2014 IPO price, even if the latest equity raise is being completed at the same price as the IPO.

If AMT-130 is approved, uniQure will by no means become profitable overnight, as this is a complex, likely extremely expensive therapy that involves brain surgery, so patients may well be cautious about undergoing treatment, and insurers potentially reluctant to reimburse.

With that said, we may speculate that thousands of HD patients will be attracted by the 80% reduction in disease progression exhibited in clinical studies and be prepared to undergo surgery. Unlike Glybera, it seems unlikely that AMT-130 would be unable to attract enough patients to be a commercial success.

Furthermore, if AMT-130 is approved it would be third drug approval secured by the company, validating its viral vector approach once again, and there are three other clinical stage drugs in uniQure's pipeline, including candidates targeting SOD1-ALS, fabry disease, and mesial temporal lobe epilepsy.

We may ask why no members of the "Big Pharma" community have partnered with uniQure on AMT-130, given its apparent superiority to current standards of care and market opportunity, and I can't provide a satisfactory answer to that question, as an asset like AMT-130 would seemingly be a perfect match for a neuroscience giant like Biogen, for example.

Perhaps it is the case that management want to go it alone with AMT-130, and who could blame them? Since IPO, the company has shown it can successfully develop useful and effective gene therapy candidates, and in the case of Hemgenix, earn a substantial amount from them, albeit mainly via milestone payments, royalties, and sales of royalties.

In July last year, uniQure agreed to the sale of its Lexington, MA manufacturing facility to Genezen, which it says, "is expected to eliminate approximately 65% or 300 roles across the organization and reduce recurring cash burn by $70 million per year." The company has long-term debt of $477m, and no near-term revenue streams to rely on, but there is plenty of cash to fund a commercial launch of AMT-130, perhaps in early 2026, if approved.

Ultimately, based on the totality of evidence I have looked at, although the path to approval could take some time, as it irons out regulatory requirements and manufacturing considerations with the FDA, I am bullish on uniQure, and see a reasonably risk-free path to a doubling of the company's valuation over time.

Of course, I can't offer any guarantees that is what will happen, but objectively speaking, this is one of the more clear-cut bull cases I have come across studying biotech companies and their prospects for 2025 / 2026.

data.title: ALPEK Is Strong In The Down Cycle And Remains A Buy
data.description:
ALPEK, S.A.B. de C.V. (OTCPK:ALPKF) (“Alpek”) released its 4Q24 results yesterday, with the stock shedding more than 20% after the release.

The market reacted negatively to the company's guidance for FY25, showing lower EBITDA than in FY24, based on a bearish or conservative view of the chemicals cycle.

Despite the challenging conditions, Alpek is expected to generate about $200 million in net income (on a CAPEX versus D&A basis) versus a market cap of $1.4 billion (MXN 28 billion). This sounds attractive at a 14% earnings yield on a cyclical thrust, with the potential to earn as much as $400 million (almost 30% yield) on a cycle average. I maintain my Strong Buy on Alpek.

Recap

I started recommending Alpek in October 2024. That article contains more information on the company's footprint, but here is a small recap.

Alpek is a leading North American producer of PTA and PET resins and products. The company maintains cost leadership (profitable even during the current negative cycle), has facilities on both sides of the border (the US and Mexico), and has low and manageable leverage (Net Debt/EBITDA of 3x on the current low cycle), with interest costs of 1/3 of EBITDA.

4Q24 results

Flat EBITDA: The company's adjusted EBITDA TTM did not improve from 3Q24, cutting short an originally positive trend seen since 2Q24.

The reason was that margins for PET and PTA products remain pressured in both Asian (via China overcapacity) and North American markets (via Asian overcapacity and also local overcapacity).

The only market that is showing improvements in margins YOY is EPS (polystyrene), where North American prices are decoupling significantly from Asia's.

Freight advantage shrinking: Most of Alpek's products are shipped via container freight across oceans. This means Asian competition (Alpek produces locally for the most part) becomes less competitive with higher freight costs. This helped the company somewhat in 2024, but the company now expects lower freight prices for 2025.

Lower EBITDA guidance: Mainly moved by still oversupplied markets, and projecting the freight advantage to decrease, the company is guiding for lower EBITDA in 2025 ($625 million versus $699 million adjusted in FY24).

Conservative outlook: Further, during the earnings call, the company's management said that a change in the cycle might take a couple of years. This means current margins will remain depressed.

On the one hand, this is negative (and probably also weighed on the company's shares), but on the other hand, the more the cycle lasts, the more supply leaves the market, and the more violent a recovery will be. Alpek can survive the current cycle because it maintains profitability, and it maintains an investment grade rating on its debt.

Priority is deleveraging, then dividends and opp M&A: The company said that it would prioritize reducing its net debt leverage ratio to 2.5x from the current 3x. With lower EBITDA, that would require paying down debt or adding cash. This will probably go against paying dividends this year, although the company did not discard the possibility.

I think this strategy makes sense. If the company believes the cycle will last more, it is good to accumulate resources (either pay debt or accumulate cash) to buy cheap assets when some producers are too strained. The company said it is always considering M&A, but will act only under adequate conditions.

Direct tariff risk is low: Management commented that only 5% of Mexican production is exported to the US. Local production serves the US market. This is a good defense against tariffs. On the other hand, if Mexico's economy suffers because of tariffs, demand in the country will decrease. Mexico represents about 1/3 of sales for the company.

Valuation remains attractive

Alpek had a very good performance since I recommended it, going from MXN 13 per share in October to MXN 16 up to 4Q24 earnings. However, after the drop, it is back to MXN 13 per share.

At these prices, the stock has a market cap of MXN 28 billion, or about $1.4 billion at current exchange rates.

In a conservative outlook for a challenging cycle period like FY25, Alpek expects an EBITDA of $625 million and to invest $150 million in CAPEX ($100 million maintenance and sustaining, $50 million growth). The company's interest cost is $200 million per year on its $1.8 billion in net debt.

The above means we could expect, after 25% taxes, that Alpek can generate about $210 million in net income (replacing CAPEX for D&A, D&A being about $200 million).

That represents a 14% yield on the current market cap, which seems attractive considering this is the yield of a very negative period.

The company commented on the 3Q24 earnings call that cycle-average EBITDA should be close to $900 million, or $412 million in net income, after removing the same figures above. That would represent a yield of almost 30%.

Alpek offers a good yield on a cycle-bottom and a great yield on a cycle-average basis. The company is well financed, it is profitable at the bottom, and is preparing to allocate capital when the time comes. For these reasons, I believe Alpek continues to be a Buy at current prices.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Hims & Hers Health: The Ultimate Pick For The Next Decade
data.description:
Introduction

I’ve written 68 stock articles in eight years for Seeking Alpha.

Twelve of them have been about Hims & Hers Health, Inc. (NYSE:HIMS).

We’re going to be talking about percentages a lot in this article, so here’s the first one: that’s 18% of all my articles – 128 pages dedicated to one company (151 once you finish this piece).

On November 30th, 2022, Seeking Alpha launched a competition for analysts to present their “Top 2023 Pick.”

I spent the next month crafting what, I hoped, would become my Magnum Opus – a 20 page, nonstop attack on every superficial argument against my Hims thesis over the years. My facts were punches, and I didn’t miss any. I wanted to write an article so irrefutable that I could have hung it on my wall like a diploma.

Unfortunately, I didn’t place in the competition.

So here we are, three years later.

Except this time, instead of Hims being “The Ultimate Pick for 2023,” (since it's only returned almost 1,000% since I wrote that article), how about I make it ‘The Ultimate Pick for the Next Decade.’

Maybe I'll have better luck catching everyone's attention this time.

Thesis

I want you to set price aside for the rest of this article. Imagine Hims has no stock price.

Hims & Hers Health is a Telehealth company growing at a blistering pace, yet it has only scratched the surface of a massive total addressable market.

What's my definition of blistering pace? That would be a 95.1% CAGR over six years since inception.

What's my definition of a huge total addressable market? That would be 200 million people across four major healthcare categories and dozens of large subsets.

Simply put, there’s a whole lotta ballgame left – and I'd know considering I play baseball professionally. After thousands of games, I have a deep sense of how long a decade feels. Any parents out there currently enduring six-hour Little League doubleheaders?

I also know what it feels like to be caught between millennial and Gen-Z behaviors, having been born right on the generational border. One thing is clear: both generations avoid in-person meetings whenever possible.

Here’s a personal example: I run an investment advisory firm, DocShah Financial. Many young clients live nearby, yet they overwhelmingly prefer virtual meetings from the comfort of their own home (and pajamas).

Here’s an observational example: More time is spent on online gaming, social media, and FaceTime than on in-person gatherings.

Why would healthcare be any different?

When Hims was valued at under $1 billion, I just sat there and watched its stock price precipitously fall for two years, despite its fundamentals improving every quarter. I had no choice but to turn to an older, highly respected colleague of mine for wisdom.

He emailed back:

That brings us to Hims. I found Hims after my first Teladoc appointment. I see this stark difference between Millenials/Gen Z and Gen X and Boomers. You guys never learned human interaction. Cell phones made it not really necessary. What do we get from that? We get a bunch of anxious/nervous people that still need health care, but they 100% would choose to do it not in person when applicable.

My Hims investment was born out of an introverted pattern I saw. It’s a psychology shift from one generation to another. And of course, you mix in all the financials and TAM, and blah, blah…

When I see Hims, I see a possible 20-billion-dollar company. And I don’t think the market gets on board until they see profitability. And that might mean one whole year of profitability so they can use P/E. Don’t ever forget that. Your ability to value cash flow is only known by less than one percent of the world. Most investors are using P/E, patterns and God knows what.

This is someone who makes me sound like an amateur investor.

He isn’t a baseball player, though. So, that was his way of saying:

“There’s a whole lotta ballgame left.”

Enjoy the piece.

The Piece

If Hims is the ultimate pick for the next decade, I guess we have plenty of bases to cover, huh? But first, let's play a little catch (up).

Hims will report Q4 2024 and FY 2024 earnings post-market on Monday, February 24th, 2025. Here’s what management has guided:

Q4 2024 Guidance

  • Revenue: $465 million to $470 million.
  • Adjusted EBITDA: $50 million to $55 million (11-12% margin).

FY 2024 Guidance

  • Revenue: $1.460 billion to $1.465 billion.
  • Adjusted EBITDA: $173 million to $178 million (12% margin).

Both revenue estimates will be wrong.

Real-time credit card data from Bloomberg, first spotted by @Himhouse on X (sourced from a Discord member), suggests that Hims’ revenue for both Q4 2024 and FY 2024 will exceed management’s guidance.

The data above shows that:

  • Analysts expect online revenue YOY growth at 67%
  • Actual credit card data shows 107% YOY growth.

Note:* Credit card data has underestimated YOY growth by an average of 12.7 percentage points over the past five quarters. To factor in this adjustment, the original poster took the consensus analyst growth rate of 80% and marked it down by 12.7% to reach 67%.

This credit card data is for Q1 2025, but unless consumers suddenly started swiping at an unprecedented rate on January 1st, this growth likely began at some point in Q4 2024.

That means Q4 revenue could easily surpass $500 million, pushing FY 2024 revenue to $1.5 billion – exactly as I predicted three years ago back on December 21, 2021 (LINK).

The growth I predicted is playing out – it's about to get fun.

How fun?

How about 25 home runs in a row fun.

100 Baggers Perspective on Sales

“100 Baggers” is a book authored by Christopher Mayer that delves into the shared traits of stocks which have gone up 100-fold. As I read through the book, a sense of déjà vu struck me numerous times as Hims checked the same boxes as these legendary 100-baggers of the past; so much so, that I wrote an entire article analyzing Hims through the lens of Mayer's framework for identifying 100-baggers.

Sales growth is one of the most telling factors, as Mayer explains:

There is no way around it. Almost all of the businesses in the 100-bagger study were substantially bigger businesses at the end than when they began.

So, you need growth-and lots of it. But not just any growth. You want value-added growth. You want "good growth." If a company doubles its sales, but also doubles the shares outstanding, that's no good. You want to focus on growth in sales and earnings per share.

Likewise, if a company generates a lot of extra sales by cutting its prices and driving down its return on equity, that may not be the kind of growth you're looking for. You want to find a business that has lots of room to expand-it's what drives those reinvestment opportunities.

Does Hims share this characteristic? It certainly does.

Hims’ sales are projected to nearly double this year, going from $872 million to $1.52 billion, while during the same period, the share count has remained essentially constant from 209 million to 218 million.

Where will future growth come from?

In no particular order.

Hers & GLP-1

It's no secret where Becky goes, her friends go.

With so much focus on men’s health, it's easy to overlook the sleeping giant: the Hers side of the brand. If Hers can successfully capture the female market, the opportunity is massive.

But how does Hers win over Becky?

Beyond dermatology and potentially customized makeup (more on both later), GLP-1s provide a natural gateway. For many women looking to lose weight, this could be their first step into Hers ecosystem – one that seamlessly transitions into other categories. Let’s break it down.

First, what is a GLP-1?

My father is a physician, so I asked him to provide the following perspective.

GLP-1 (Glucagon-Like Peptide-1) is a naturally occurring hormone that helps regulate blood sugar, digestion, and appetite. In simple terms:

  • When you eat, your gut releases GLP-1.
  • It signals the pancreas to release insulin, lowering blood sugar.
  • It slows digestion, keeping food in the stomach longer.
  • It tells the brain that you’re full, reducing appetite.

GLP-1 medications (like Ozempic, Wegovy, Mounjaro, and Zepbound) are synthetic versions that last longer in the body. They’re primarily used to treat diabetes and aid in weight loss by mimicking natural GLP-1 functions.

Second, how does a patient typically obtain a GLP-1?

Traditionally, a patient gets a prescription from their doctor and picks up the medication from a pharmacy. Only companies holding patents – Novo Nordisk for semaglutide (Wegovy and Ozempic) and Eli Lilly for tirzepatide (Mounjaro and Zepbound) – can legally manufacture these drugs.

Third, why do compounding pharmacies exist?

Compounding pharmacies exist to tailor medications for individual needs. They might remove allergens, alter dosages, or change a pill into a liquid form. They also step in when there are drug shortages, allowing them to legally produce medications under specific laws (which vary by jurisdiction). This personalization can be a major advantage, particularly when it comes to medications like GLP-1s.

The list price for most brand-name GLP-1 drugs is between $1,000 and $1,400, though patients often pay $500–$600. Hims and Hers, by leveraging compounding, offers them for around $200 (in fact, they’re on sale now for $165).

Hims Compounding Pharmacies

Hims & Hers owns (key word) two compounding pharmacies, both a 503A and a 503B, which serve different purposes:

  • 503A Compounding Pharmacies (Ohio & Arizona): Custom-make medications for individual patients based on prescriptions. These are smaller-scale operations tailored to specific needs and regulated by state pharmacy boards.
  • 503B Outsourcing Facilities (e.g., MedisourceRx in California): Manufacture medications in bulk, particularly sterile compounds like GLP-1 injections, for hospitals and clinics. These facilities follow strict FDA regulations.

Analogy: A 503A pharmacy is like a custom bakery making cakes to order, while a 503B pharmacy is a commercial bakery mass-producing them for grocery stores.

Why does this matter for Hers?

Hers’ approach to capturing the female market seems to be focused on two key categories: hormonal therapy (which includes GLP-1s) and dermatology (and hopefully personalized makeup one day). Acquiring both 503A and 503B pharmacies wasn’t just about reducing margins through vertical integration – it was about differentiating Hims and Hers from competitors by offering personalized, lower-cost medications.

This is a major competitive advantage.

However, competitive advantages alone don’t get Becky’s attention (and neither does your loud car, for the record). So how does Hers attract women?

The word-of-mouth flywheel

If you haven’t noticed, women vary in ethnicity, body type, and genetic makeup. A personalized GLP-1 treatment tailored to an individual would yield better results at a lower price (since Hims owns its compounding pharmacies). If a woman successfully loses weight using Hims and Hers’ personalized GLP-1s, she’s likely to post her transformation on social media and tell her friends.

That’s phase one: organic, viral word-of-mouth marketing. You can actually see this playing out in real time based on the Reddit forum growth, r/HersWeightloss.

Phase two is cross-selling these new customers on Hims’ other offerings, particularly dermatology (and hopefully personalized makeup in the future). Once women enter the ecosystem, their lifetime value increases substantially.

The GLP-1 Cliff: Is It a Threat?

I’m turning to my wise colleague from earlier for the following perspective.

This is Amazon’s (AMZN) all-time chart.

The company’s single biggest drop in its history, a 95% decline from $113 per share to $5, occurred when the dot-com bubble burst.

If you didn’t know when the dot-com bubble burst, could you point it out on this chart?

No.

So now, which do you think is worse? The dot-com bubble or the GLP-1 cliff?

This chart is a timeless lesson in how powerful compounding is – in the end, nothing matters but your ROIC. Amazon reinvested their capital at such high returns, that you can’t even see that 95% decline in their stock price anymore.

Investing requires perspective. If you own Hims over the next thirty years, there will be a dozen more “GLP-1 cliffs.” When they show up, come back to this chart.

Amazon’s dot-com bubble is not even a blip on the timeline.

And neither will be Hims’ GLP-1 cliff.

MedMatch & AI

The giant sleeping right next to Hers is MedMatch, Hims’ AI-driven machine learning software that the market hasn’t even begun to discount.

MedMatch is designed to optimize treatment recommendations by suggesting the most effective medications, dosages, and formulations based on millions of past cases.

How will MedMatch drive growth?

The most obvious impact is higher retention – if patients get better results, they’re more likely to stick with Hims, but the real driver is cost savings.

AI-powered decision-making means you pay a doctor for one visit instead of two. While complex cases require more nuance, this is a practical solution for a telehealth company focused on routine care – one that should drive significant savings.

Even a 1–3% reduction in operating expenses would have an outsized impact on free cash flow. However, MedMatch’s full potential will only be realized when Hims amasses billions of data points – something that will take time.

At this stage, the key to MedMatch’s success is hiring a highly capable Chief Technology Officer that can oversee building a scalable AI healthcare machine learning model. With the right CTO and budget in place, Hims would scale much faster than the market anticipates.

The Board & CTO

Speaking of the CTO, let's take a look at Hims' board of directors and mention some notable people.

Side note: You can gauge a company’s ambition by its board members. Usually boards are “phoned in,” being filled with the generic suspects, but in this case, each member’s unique skills are clearly aligned with the company’s long-term mission. The best way I can describe it is the board members feel deliberate.

Dr. Patrick Carroll

Dr. Carroll serves as Chief Medical Officer at Hims, and gets first mention. He, along with Dr. Toby Cosgrove (former Chief Executive of the Cleveland Clinic), are two of the most important board members simply because they are the oldest. They have more business, medical, and life experience than most of the Hims executives.

Why is Dr. Carroll especially important? He takes a more active role as CMO and appears to be a fatherly figure to the team. This is on prime display, by the way, each time he chooses to showcase some restraint in not immediately exercising his options the second they vest (cough, cough Soleil).

Kåre Schultz

Mr. Schultz was an executive at Novo Nordisk (NVO) and Teva Pharmaceutical (TEVA), the latter of which is the world’s largest generic drug manufacturer.

What does this mean? He streamlined Teva to focus on generics and specialty medications, and expanded Novo Nordisk’s diabetes portfolio over his 25-year tenure there. He’s at Hims to do the same thing.

Deb Autor

Ms. Autor was a Deputy Commissioner at the FDA who brings deep knowledge of domestic regulatory frameworks.

What does this mean? She will help guide Hims’ regulatory strategy and enhance its safety and quality standards. Andrew Dudum is making it abundantly clear Hims is a healthcare company, not a cosmetic wellness brand.

Anja Manuel

Ms. Manuel is a foreign policy expert and former U.S. government official who specializes in guiding U.S. companies through complex international regulatory landscapes.

What does this mean? It means Andrew Dudum wants to eventually take the company across the oceans; and in order to so, he’s going to need a highly talented board member with experience in scaling businesses internationally and regulating them overseas. Enter Anja.

Side note: Has anyone read Anja’s resume? It’s unbelievable – she served as an attorney, investment banker, U.S. Diplomat, lecturer, authored books, has degrees from Stanford and Harvard, and she’s on multiple boards.

David Wells

Mr. Wells is the former CFO of Netflix.

Why is this important? He scaled Netflix’s subscription based-revenue model, optimized cash flow, and drove profitability during a period of massive growth. His expertise in managing high-growth, capital-intensive businesses will be invaluable as Hims transitions from a marketing-heavy cost structure to a more operationally efficient, machine-learning-driven model.

Christopher Payne

Mr. Payne was the CEO of Tinder.

Why is he crucial? He knows how to grow a digital platform, especially one based on subscriptions and AI-driven user engagement. That’s all Tinder is… it’s just algorithms. MedMatch is the same… it’s just algorithms.

The Need for a Chief Technology Officer (CTO)

Hims needs a Chief Technology Officer (CTO) to lead the shift from high marketing spend to machine learning-driven efficiency. As machine learning becomes the core of Hims’ evolution, it will enhance patient outcomes and reduce costs. To realize the vision of becoming a fully AI-driven healthcare provider, integrating genetic data, wearable tracking, and predictive analytics for personalized treatments, the company must hire a machine learning architect as CTO. This critical role will ensure MedMatch evolves into a fully optimized AI-driven platform, accelerating Hims' transformation.

Personalized solutions

Another large future growth opportunity lies in personalized solutions, which have potential to be a huge competitive advantage.

Hims & Hers recognizes this, which is why it’s doubling down on personalization with two new customer-led programs:

  1. Hims & Hers Health Collective – A community-driven ambassador program where customers can connect, share their health journeys, and get early access to new products.
  2. Hims & Hers Community Member Council – A hand-selected group of 15 customers who provide direct feedback to shape future products and services.

Hims' personalized offerings should continue to steal market share from competitors like Teladoc (TDOC), 23&Me, LifeMD, Ro, etc.

We'll get to a competitor comparison later in the article.

Acquisitions/Partnerships

Hims’ management has proven to be excellent stewards of capital. Here are two home runs they’ve hit.

Apostrophe

Apostrophe has provided Hims with a personalized dermatology platform, while also serving as a factory and warehousing hub, strengthening Hims’ vertical integration. Looking ahead, I’d love to see Hims expand into a custom makeup line, whether through Apostrophe’s growth into that market, an acquisition, or in-house development. Providing full personalized beauty for customers at the same place they buy their custom skincare products would be a natural fit and a total game changer.

Trybe Labs

Hims just acquired Trybe Labs, an at-home lab testing facility, with its cash on hand. That means that “Hims can test for a wide range of critical biomarkers across heart, hormone, liver, thyroid and prostate – helping to proactively identify individual risks for disease.”

This acquisition has three monumental implications:

    1. It signifies Hims’ official entry into the statin, diabetes, and hormonal therapy markets. These are gargantuan markets.
    2. It opens the door to a brand new and growing market: biohackers and sportifs.
    3. MedMatch can now aggregate vast amounts of crucial data points for its AI machine learning database, allowing for the creation of highly personalized medicines tailored to each patient’s DNA.

If Tesla is a data management company, so is Hims.

Apple (I’m Calling This Now)

I can see it happening – Apple partnering with Hims to allow iPhone users to seamlessly access and review their personal healthcare data from Hims directly within the iOS Health app, which needs bolstering. After all, their branding is very similar. This integration would provide users with a comprehensive health snapshot, making healthcare more accessible and personalized.

If this actually ever happens, you heard it first from me, Raul Shah, first.

Valuation

In my rough draft, I had three pages of discounted cash flow, or DCF, valuations – a base, bear, bull, and hyper-bull case.

I deleted them all.

The reality is neither I nor you need a DCF to value Hims at this point. The forecasts would be pure guess work due to how quickly the company is growing, evolving, investing, etc.

Here’s how I think about Hims:

It’s grown at a CAGR of 70% in the past two years, yet it hasn’t even captured 1% of the U.S. telehealth market, let alone any noticeable amount of the global telehealth market.

Can Hims reasonably grow at 30% for the next decade?

Considering the current tiny market penetration, gigantic TAM, and executive talent, yes.

Can Hims achieve a 25% net income margin by year ten?

Considering the 81% gross margins and operational leverage MedMatch would provide, adding in the company is vertically integrating, yes.

What will the share count be in a decade?

Beats me, but I bet it will be a chunk higher. Let’s assume 300 million shares.

Could the marketplace a 25 P/E on the stock?

Considering companies with SAAS like gross margins that compound 30% + a year trade at higher-than-average multiples, yes.

What would the stock price be?

Trick question… I told you to set aside price. Don’t think I forgot.

What would the business be worth?

If $1.46 billion in revenue grew at 30%, then top line would be $20 billion in ten years. At 20% margins, $4 billion would fall to the bottom line. At 25 times earnings, Hims would be a $100 billion company. Assuming the share count grew to 300 million, each share would be worth $333.

Is Hims a potential 100-bagger for those of us who bought at its $3 lows? Yes.

How long could that take? I have no idea.

In Christopher Mayer’s book, he researched the average time required for a stock to become a 100-bagger: 26 years.

Another interesting chart to look at concerns how long these stocks took to become 100-baggers. The average time was 26 years. That was also the median.

In other words, don’t hold your breath.

Mainstream Growth

For years, Hims flew under the radar. I still remember the first time Jim Cramer mentioned Hims on his CNBC show, ‘Mad Money.’ It was barely a minute-long shoutout, but it felt like we had finally “made it” despite the title of the video ironically being “Cramer's Lightening Round: Hims & Hers Health is Not a Buy.”

Followers/Rankings

Back when I started writing on Seeking Alpha, Hims had fewer than 6,000 followers. Fast-forward a few years, and that number has grown to 24,000. The same growth is evident on StockTwits. Meanwhile, the Hims and Hers apps are now going head-to-head, ranking #3 and #2, respectively, in the iOS App Store’s “Medical category.”

Google Trends

Over the past five years, Google Trends data shows a steady upward trajectory for both “Hims” and “Hers.” The brand's Super Bowl ad drove a remarkable 650% surge in website traffic and sign-ups. Forbes ranked it as the second most impactful ad of the night, while EDO reported that it generated 8x more engagement than the average Super Bowl ad, making it the #5 ad overall.

Institutions

Institutional ownership is up tenfold over the past five years. In fact, I went back and found a screenshot of Hims’ ownership structure from May 9th, 2023 compared to today. See below:

Institutions typically dislike when retail investors own the majority of a company's shares. It creates challenges for them in securing favorable prices on their investments, which is crucial because they deal with much larger sums of money. For example, a 1% price movement, which is inconsequential for the average person, can result in a significant profit or loss for institutional investors, making them extremely sensitive to the price of each block they purchase. When retail investors control most of the outstanding shares, it throws a wrench in their plans. Unfortunately, it appears that retail investors have sold about 2/3 of their shares, and those shares have now been absorbed by institutions.

Below are some firms that own shares for your own information:

Competition

Hims has six primary competitors in the telehealth space, five of which are public and one private (Ro). For the sake of this analysis, I’ll focus on Teladoc to give us a perspective on how Hims is performing compared to a notable competitor. As for other companies like 23andMe, UpHealth, American Well, and LifeMD, they simply aren’t serious competitors to Hims anymore, so we won’t waste time examining them.

Company 1 vs. Company 2

The same way we’ve removed price from the equation throughout this article, let's take names out of the equation to see a clear picture.

  • One company has seen their revenue growth almost completely dry up, while the other is poised for 90% YOY growth.
  • One company has consistently seen gross margins decline, while the other has never had a year without expanding gross margins.
  • One company’s debt-to-capital ratio has ballooned to 50%, while the other has doesn’t have a dime of long-term debt.
  • One company generates one-third the revenue with its assets compared to the other.
  • One company takes, on average, 25 days to turn its inventory into cash, while the other company actually gets paid 35 days before spending a dime.
  • And as a bonus: One company lost $1 billion in the past year, and around $15 billion over the past three years, while the other hasn’t lost that much in total over its lifetime – and has recently become profitable.

In each example, the first company is Teladoc and the second company is Hims.

For the first time, on page 19/23, we can introduce price.

If there is one thing you take away from this article, make it this:

A company is worth its future cash flows, and over enough time, nothing deters that; not short reports, not headlines, and certainly not analyst price targets.

That’s why Teladoc’s stock price has fallen by (94%) since January 3rd, 2021 and Hims’ stock price has risen by 300%.

External Catalysts

We covered internal growth earlier, but here are two external catalysts that could add more fuel to the fire.

S&P 400 Inclusion

A significant catalyst for Hims would be its inclusion in the S&P 400 during its rebalancing that will occur in early March.

The S&P MidCap 400 tracks the performance of 400 mid-sized U.S. companies, positioning it between the S&P 500 (large-cap) and S&P 600 (small-cap) in terms of market capitalization. Getting included in this index would lead to substantial institutional buying, and Hims seems to meet the necessary criteria for inclusion. The S&P 400 rebalancing is driven by five key factors:

1) Market Capitalization

  • Requirement: Mid-cap range, $7.4 billion to $20.5 billion.
  • Hims' Status: Hims' current market capitalization is $13 billion, comfortably within this range.

2) Minimum Investable Weight Factor (IWF)

  • Requirement: An IWF of at least 0.10.
  • Hims' Status: Hims' IWF is estimated to be around 0.37% based on its 84% free float.

3) Financial Viability

  • Requirement: Positive earnings in the most recent quarter and the past four quarters combined.
  • Hims' Status: Hims has posted four consecutive profitable quarters, with expectations for a fifth in Q1 2024.

4) Liquidity & Trading Volume

  • Requirement: Sufficient liquidity and trading volume.
  • Hims' Status: Hims' average daily trading volume is around 15 million shares, which is ample liquidity.

5) Sector Alignment

  • Requirement: Proper diversification within the index.
  • Hims' Status: Hims would be the only Telehealth company included in the index.

S&P 400 Fund Impact

As you can see, Hims meets all five criteria to be potentially included in the index. Several large funds track the S&P 400, which would lead to automatic buying of Hims shares if it’s added. Rough estimates suggest that inclusion could drive $150 billion in fund buying. Given Hims' index weight of 0.37%, approximately $555 million worth of Hims shares would need to be purchased. At a price of $60, that translates to roughly 9.25 million shares, or about 5% of its float.

Short Squeeze

Speaking of shares, Hims still has a large short interest at 33%, which represents over 58 million shares. I've written about this in a previous article, but the trick to shorting stocks isn't shorting stocks everyone knows is overvalued – the trick is shorting stocks is figuring out when everyone will care the stock is overvalued. That's why the smart shorts wait until it's all but over before shorting.

Why? Because shorting a stock at $60 gives you the same return as shorting it at $5 if the stock goes to zero. I'm not suggesting Hims is overvalued, but I am surprised that, given the laundry list of upcoming catalysts, shorts would target this stock over others.

It's not just me who feels this way, by the way; even the options market realizes this and is pricing in this optimism. For the first time, there are $5 intervals on the strikes, and the option chain now goes up to one increment under triple digits.

Chain Reaction, Squeeze Spiral, Whiplash Effect, Shortpocalypse

Call it what you want, but what would be really comical is if Hims has a phenomenal earnings report causing the stock price to rise, thus triggering shorts to cover, only to then lead into the potential S&P 400 inclusion, which would again cause the price to rise, which would trigger the shorts to cover.

It would be hilarious.

You could effectively wind up having a two week-long short squeeze.

Risks

Let’s not get carried away, though. There are plenty of risks involved in buying shares of Hims.

Semaglutide coming off the FDA’s shortage list

One of the primary risks on investors’ minds is the potential revenue decline when Semaglutide comes off the FDA's shortage list. This could impact Hims' ability to compound the medicine. While this presents a short-term challenge, the company’s diverse product categories and total addressable market remain strong. Looking ahead, a potential revenue dip next year may seem insignificant in the long term. However, depending on your investment horizon, this could represent a substantial risk.

Share dilution

The executive team at Hims is not shy about compensating themselves generously. The share buyback program implemented last year has essentially been completely negated by significant stock-based compensation (SBC). As a result, investors will need to accept the likelihood that Hims will balloon its share count over time, which will dilute all its future earnings.

Big pharma

As Hims gains market share, Big Pharma’s influence in Washington is likely to become more pronounced. This could result in a range of lawsuits, some of which may threaten profitability. More concerning, however, would be the potential delays these legal battles could cause to Hims' growth and market penetration. The risk here lies not just in direct costs, but in slowing the company’s overall trajectory.

Lawsuit

Related to the previous point, Hims must be cautious in how it prescribes medication. While the size of a potential lawsuit may not be the primary concern, the reputational damage from mis-prescriptions could have significant consequences. Any scandal that undermines the company’s core initiative of “modern, technology-driven healthcare” could erode public trust. If this happens, Hims could be perceived as a fad or, worse, an unregulated and risky player in the healthcare space.

Regulation

As Hims continues to expand its market footprint, increased regulatory scrutiny is a natural progression. Given the industry’s growth and profitability, it’s likely only a matter of time before the government imposes stricter regulations on telehealth. This could increase compliance costs and put downward pressure on operating margins.

Market euphoria

It's important to acknowledge that the overall market appears to be trading at elevated levels relative to historical averages. For instance, the S&P 500 is currently trading at a P/E ratio of 30x, compared to its historical average of 17-18x. If the broader market corrects, it could result in a sharp reversal in Hims' share price, as the stock would likely be caught up in any downturn.

10-K risks

For the company’s provided set of risks, please click here.

Takeaway

As I type these final words, I’m letting out a sigh of relief. This article took me weeks to research, write, and edit. I truly hope you found it informative, but more importantly, learned a lesson you can apply to future investments.

At this point, there’s nothing left for me to add or summarize – I’ve given you everything I have. I’ll just leave you with this…

We think what we’re building is a $10-20 billion company in the next few years.

Andrew Dudum was wrong. He’s building a potential $100 billion company.

I am not suggesting HIMS will be a 100-bagger stock.

I was wrong. I bought a potential 100-bagger.

What’s the most Gen-Z/Millennial way I can end this piece?

I guess by acknowledging, our relationship is also through a screen.

How ironic.

P.S.

I typed in “Teladoc board” and this is what came up on Google. I did the same thing for Hims (“Hims Board”), and this is what showed up. Interpret these results as you wish.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Walmart Sinks On Cautious Outlook
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify.

Stock falls with guidance below expectations. (0:15) OpenAI hits 400 million weekly users. (3:41) Amazon will deliver the next Bond film. (4:11)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far. Walmart’s (NYSE:WMT) future guidance is spooking investors and shares are sharply lower.

While Q4 results topped analysts’ expectations, the outlook assumes a generally stable consumer and continued pressure from its mix of products and formats globally.

The expectation for Q1 sales to be up a 3% to 4% should result in EPS between $0.57 and $0.58. That’s down 3 to 4 cents from a year ago and below the consensus estimate of $0.65. For FY26, Walmart also expects sales to improve 3%-4%, translating to $698.1 billion at the midpoint with EPS between $2.50 to $2.60 per share. This is also less than Wall Street’s expectations for EPS of $2.76 per share on $705.14 billion in sales.

However, shares did come off their lows, during the earnings call, where CEO Doug McMillan said “once you take the noise out of guidance, we’re in a great spot to start the year as we grow profits faster than sales.”

The sell side also offered support. Jefferies analyst Corey Tarlowe said: “We think WMT is setting up '25 to be a year of beats & raises (similar to '24).”

Morgan Stanley’s Simeon Gutman said: “Street estimates are likely to be revised down this morning. Similar to FY25, we would expect a path of outperformance in FY26 and ultimate EPS power exceeding the initial outlook.”

Walmart also raised its dividend by 13% to $0.235 per share.

Among other active stocks, Palantir’s (PLTR) dive in the previous session, on potential defense spending cuts, continues today as well as the stock is sinking again. Shares are currently down 20% from Tuesday’s close.

However, Wedbush analyst Dan Ives came to the company’s defense, saying: “The bears, which have hated Palantir from $12 to $120 in the last 18 months, now have found their latest 'silver bullet' negative thesis around PLTR being exposed to these budget cuts.”

“This is exactly the opposite (of) how we believe these DOD cuts will play out as in our view Palantir's unique software approach will enable the company to gain more IT budget dollars at the Pentagon....not less.”

Alibaba (BABA) is sharply up after topping quarterly consensus numbers. Key takeaways included the beats for revenue, adjusted EBITDA, and cloud intelligence segment sales.

Morgan Stanley said the earnings report strengthened its investment thesis as it made modest upward revisions to its EPS estimates following the print. Meanwhile, Jefferies highlighted that the Alibaba's International Digital Commerce Group revenue rose 32% year-over-year to smash expectations.

On the economic front, initial jobless claims rose by 5,000 to 219,000, a little more than expected.

The federal government's campaign to cut costs and fire workers doesn't appear to be affecting DC-area claims so far. About 20% of federal workers live in the metro area. Initial claims filed in the District of Columbia rose by 13 to 1,695; claims filed in Maryland dropped by 821 to 2,354, and claims filed in Virginia declined by 397 to 2,805.

Samuel Tombs, chief U.S. economist at Pantheon Macro, says: "any laid off federal employees likely will file for claims in the coming weeks, particularly if the layoffs broaden out, as seems likely. Next week’s numbers likely will also receive an uplift from extreme winter weather in parts of the country."

In other news of note, OpenAI had 400 million weekly active users for ChatGPT as of February. It’s a 33% increase from the 300 million weekly active users as of Dec. 4, 2024. The company had more than 2 million paying business users, compared to 1 million as of Sept. 5.

Apple's (AAPL) integration of OpenAI's AI model started in December last year. Apple Intelligence relies on a mix of Apple’s own AI technology and a collaboration with OpenAI.

Amazon MGM Studios (AMZN) is taking over the creative development of the James Bond franchise, with longtime producers Barbara Broccoli and Michael G. Wilson stepping back. Amazon MGM, Wilson and Broccoli have formed a new joint venture to house the intellectual property rights will remain co-owners of 007. Financial terms were not disclosed.

Amazon gained distribution rights to all the James Bond movies after it bought Metro Goldwyn Mayer for $8.5 billion back in 2022. With the current announcement, the company will also be responsible for creative control and production of future Bond films.

Possible upcoming Amazon-Bond titles already in development: Dr. No Returns, From Russia With Free Shipping, You Only Click Twice, On Her Majesty's Same-Day Service and Live and Let Prime.

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, Goldman chief economist Jan Hatzius says changes in AI-related capital expenditures could see just limited near-term implications, despite the DeepSeek scares.

He says competition could “catalyze higher levels of real hardware spending if it pushes incumbents to invest more to maintain their lead.” Goldman Sachs analysts have, in fact, increased their estimates of 2025 AI-related investment on net over the last quarter.

Capex expectations for 2025 were revised up by 5% for S&P 1500 (SPTM) companies over the last quarter but were only revised up by 2% for companies with broad exposure to tariffs and revised down by 1% for companies with a high reported share of sales to Canada, Mexico, and China.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
